title: Vision-Language Notes
text:
These are working notes from my PhD research on vision-language models. My background is in physics and applied mathematics (complex systems), and these notes document the technical foundations I am building as I go deeper into computer vision, multimodal models, and representation learning.
This is not a polished textbook. It is a living document – updated as I read, think, and get things wrong.

title: Vision-Language Notes
text:
Chapters

Stage 0 – Curriculum overview
Stage 1 – How neural networks learn
Stage 2 – Convolutional neural networks

title: Vision-Language Notes
text:
Background
PhD candidate in computer vision and vision-language models. BSc Physics, MSc Modelling for Science and Engineering (complex systems track).

title: Stage 1: How Neural Networks Learn
text:
Nielsen, Neural Networks and Deep Learning, chapters 1 and 2. http://neuralnetworksanddeeplearning.com
Estimated time: 4-6 hours.

title: Stage 1: How Neural Networks Learn
text:
Nielsen, Neural Networks and Deep Learning, chapters 1 and 2. http://neuralnetworksanddeeplearning.com
Estimated time: 4-6 hours.

title: Stage 1: How Neural Networks Learn
text:
Key concepts to watch for

Forward pass: what happens to an input as it moves through the network
Loss function: what the network is trying to minimize, and what that means geometrically
Gradient descent: walking downhill in a very high-dimensional landscape
Backpropagation: the chain rule, applied systematically

title: Stage 1: How Neural Networks Learn
text:
Checkpoint
After finishing chapters 1 and 2, answer these in your own words:

What is a neural network actually doing during a forward pass?
What does “training” mean geometrically?
Does backpropagation feel mysterious after reading Nielsen, or does it reduce to something familiar?

(answers below)

title: Stage 1: How Neural Networks Learn
text:
My answers

A neural network is composed of individual processing units called neurons, organised in layers. Each neuron applies a linear transformation to its inputs (via weights and biases) followed by a nonlinearity such as a sigmoid. The nonlinearity is essential since, without it, stacking layers would collapse into a single linear transformation, losing all expressive power. Information flows forward through these layers, from input to output, where the final layer produces a prediction over some set of categories or values.
Training means adjusting the weights of the network to minimize a cost function, which measures the distance between the network’s predictions and the correct answers in the training set. Geometrically, this is optimization in weight space: a space with as many dimensions as there are parameters in the network. Gradient descent walks downhill in this space by computing the gradient of the cost function with respect to all weights and taking a small step in the opposite direction. Backpropagation is what makes this efficient: it applies the chain rule recursively, propagating the error signal backward through the network and reusing intermediate gradients at each layer rather than recomputing them from scratch.
Not really, it reduces to the chain rule from calculus, applied recursively across the computational graph of the network.

title: Stage 1: How Neural Networks Learn
text:
Open questions
In a perceptron with many layers, what is the algebraic relationship between each of the layers? Are they adding non-linearities?

title: Stage 1: How Neural Networks Learn
text:
Notes




flowchart LR
    D[Contents] --> A[Types of neurons]
    D[Contents] --> E[Stochastic gradient descent, primary learning mechanism]
    A[Types of neurons] --> B[Perceptron]
    A[Types of neurons] --> C[Sigmoid neuron]


Types of neurons discussed in the chapter




Perceptron



Perceptron


Where \(x_i\) is the input. Let \(w_i\) be the weight, where the binary output results in
.
One can also write the relationship as a dot product with a bias term (equivalent to the y-intercept in a linear regression model), where the threshold information gets translated to a bias term instead.
We might use particular arrangements of weights and biases to create a system which behaves like logical gate (e.g. NAND gate). However, changing the weights and biases will alter the logic behaviour between inputs and outputs, where, the weights be automaticallt adjusted, we could, theoretically, have the perceptron “learn”.


Sigmoid neuron
The perceptron carries an intrinsic problem for learning applications, since small changes can cause large changes in the output, making it difficult to learn. This is due to the step function binary nature of the perceptron. In order to solve this problem, one can define the sigmoid neuron, which is based in a sigmoid function instead of binary.


The architecture of neural networks
In general terms, there are three types of layers in a neural network: input layer, hidden layer(s), and output layer. The input layer receives the input data, the hidden layers perform computations and transformations on the data, and the output layer produces the final result.
.
Given these basic elements, one can construct architectures of neural networks with different arrangements of neurons. For instance, the one given immediately above is a feedforward neural network, meaning there are no loops present, and the information flows in one direction from input to output. There are, however, other arrangements which do include loops, called recurrent neural networks (RNNs).


A simple network to classify handwritten digits
In an image with a sequence of numbers, we could break this problem into two parts: first, identify the existance of different characters and; secondly, classify each of them as a cypher. We will focus on the latter, since the first problem becomes more evident after we have solved the first.
Take each image of a digit to be 28x28 pixels, meaning a total of 784 pixels. This corresponds to the number of neurons in the input layer, for we are analysing the level of contrast of these, between black and white (between 1 and 0). Moving forward to the hidden layer, we can experiment with different number of neurons. In this example we will have 15 neurons in the hidden layer. Finally, the output layer has 10 neurons, corresponding to the digits from 0 to 9.

title: Curriculum Overview
text: A structured path through the technical foundations of vision-language models, designed for someone coming from physics and applied mathematics.

title: Curriculum Overview
text:
Dependency structure
Each stage builds on the previous. The through-line is this: all of these models are learning to compress high-dimensional data into lower-dimensional representations. What gets preserved and what gets discarded in that compression is the technical core of most interesting questions in this space.

title: Curriculum Overview
text:
Stages

Stage 1 – How neural networks learn
Core reading: Nielsen, Neural Networks and Deep Learning, chapters 1-2. Free online
The foundation. Forward pass, loss functions, gradient descent, backpropagation.


Stage 2 – Convolutional Neural Networks
Core reading: CS231n notes on CNNs (Stanford, free online)
Spatial locality and translation equivariance as inductive biases. Convolution as learned feature detection. Residual connections.


Stage 3 – Attention and Transformers
Core reading: Vaswani et al. 2017 (Attention is All You Need) + Jay Alammar’s The Illustrated Transformer
The architecture that replaced everything else. Self-attention as a soft, differentiable, input-dependent weighted average. Vision Transformers (ViTs) as the direct precursor to CLIP’s image encoder.


Stage 4 – CLIP and vision-language models
Core reading: Radford et al. 2021 (Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision)
Contrastive learning, shared embedding spaces, zero-shot classification. Why the training data matters enormously.


Stage 5 – Evaluation and benchmarks
Core reading: Barocas, Hardt, Narayanan – Fairness and Machine Learning, selected chapters. Free online
What benchmarks actually measure. Subgroup performance. The incompatibility of fairness metrics.

title: Stage 2: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
CS231n, Convolutional Neural Networks for Visual Recognition, module on CNNs. https://cs231n.github.io/convolutional-networks
Estimated time: 2-3 hours.

title: Stage 2: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
Key concepts to watch for

Convolution: what it actually does to an image mathematically
Inductive biases: spatial locality and translation equivariance, and why they matter
Feature maps: what gets detected at each layer, and how abstraction builds up
Pooling: coarse-graining the representation
Residual connections: why depth was hard before ResNets, and what skip connections solve

title: Stage 2: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
Checkpoint
After finishing the reading, answer these in your own words:

What is a convolution doing to an image? What is the filter learning?
What does “translation equivariance” mean, and why is it a useful assumption for images?
As you go deeper in a CNN, what happens to the representation? What is gained and what is lost?
What problem do residual connections solve, and why does that matter for deep networks?

(answers below)

title: Stage 2: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
My answers

A convolutional layer extracts local features from an image by sliding a small filter across the spatial dimensions and computing a dot product at each position. Each filter learns to detect one specific pattern (an edge, a texture, a colour gradient). Stacking multiple convolutional layers produces increasing abstraction: early layers detect simple features, deeper layers detect combinations of those features into increasingly complex patterns.
Translation equivariance means that if a feature shifts position in the input image, its corresponding activation in the feature map shifts by the same amount. This is a useful assumption for images because objects can appear anywhere in the frame: a cat in the top left and a cat in the bottom right are still cats, and the filter should detect both without needing to be retrained for each location.
As you go deeper in a CNN, the representation gains abstraction and depth: more filters track more distinct and complex features simultaneously. It loses spatial resolution, as width and height shrink through pooling. Fine-grained spatial detail is traded for richer, more abstract feature representations.
Residual connections solve the vanishing gradient problem. As networks get deeper, the gradient signal shrinks as it travels backward through many layers during backpropagation, causing early layers to stop learning effectively. Skip connections give gradients a direct path backward by adding the input of a layer directly to its output, bypassing the transformation. This allows very deep networks to train effectively without degradation in performance.

title: Stage 2: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
Open questions

“3D volumes of neurons” –> what does this mean? 3D is already volume if we’re talking space coordinates. What dimensions are we talking about exaclty? What space are they living in?; Apparently by 3D they mean a tensor in 3 dimensions. For an input image the three dimensions could for instance be the width and height of an image, and the “depth” or third dimension could be the color channels (3 for RGB). So a 32x32 color image is a 32x32x3 array of numbers.
How do you choose the activation filters? Are they randomly initialised?; Yes, but with different values, since otherways they’d all be learning the same thing.

title: Stage 2: Convolutional Neural Networks
text:
Notes

Definition & Core Elements
CNNs have the same key components of the neural networks we saw before but, in this case, their loss function can be for instance an SVM or softmax and, most importantly, they assume all inputs are images. In particular, regular neural networks do not scale well to images, which is why we can have this subset of neural networks as a specialised subtype particular to the problematics commonly found in images.
CNNs have three types of layers: Convolutional Layer, Pooling Layer and Fully-Connected Layer (add ReLU as an elementwise transformation). Stacking these results in the full architecture of a CNN. As the image moves through the network, width and height shrink while depth grows, encoding increasingly abstract features. The four main layer types are:

CONV (Convolutional Layer). The core of the network. A set of small learned filters slides across the spatial dimensions of the input. At each position, it computes a dot product between the filter weights and the local patch of the image. Since weights can be any real number (positive or negative), the output can also be negative or positive. Each filter produces one 2D activation map, and stacking all of them gives the new depth dimension. Early filters learn to detect edges and textures; deeper filters detect increasingly abstract patterns.
ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit). Applied elementwise immediately after convolution. The function is simply \(f(x) = \max(0, x)\): positive values pass through unchanged, negative values are set to zero. A negative activation means the filter did not detect its feature at that location, so ReLU converts it to “nothing detected.” Without this nonlinearity, stacking layers would collapse into a single linear transformation and the network would lose expressive power.
POOL (Pooling Layer). Downsamples the spatial dimensions. The most common version is max pooling: a 2x2 window slides across each activation map and keeps only the maximum value in each patch, discarding the rest. This halves the width and height while preserving depth. Other examples are average pooling, for instance. The intuition is coarse-graining: integrating out fine-grained spatial detail to keep only the dominant signal, reducing computation and making the representation more compact.

FC (Fully-Connected Layer). At the end of the network, once the spatial dimensions have been reduced to a small size, the volume is flattened and passed through one or more fully-connected layers, exactly as in a standard neural network. The final FC layer outputs one score per class, and the class with the highest score is the prediction.
The full pipeline for a simple CNN is therefore: detect local features (CONV), introduce nonlinearity (ReLU), (repeat as many times as necessary), reduce spatial resolution (POOL), repeat until compact, then classify (FC).


Architecture Case Study
A particular case of a relevant CNN architecture is ResNet. In general, more layers tends to work better. However, one problem which arises with this is that if you just naively increase the number of layers, you end up having a higher training error. In ResNets, however, the training and testing error have a consistent improvement as you increase the number of layers.
In a resnet, there are skip connections. What that means is that, instead of computing a whole layer again, you’re just computing an addition between layer and layer without getting rid of the original layer again and again. Essentially, if you have an identity matrix, you’re just computing a changing \(\delta\) on top of it, instead of computing the whole changing matrix again and again. This makes it easier to compute with and optimise.
